seed_instruction: Write a story about a dropout who returns to school later in life to achieve their dreams.
org_constraint: Begin the story with the phrase "It was never too late."
new_constraint: Avoid using the phrase "too late" in the opening sentence.
instruction: Write a story about a dropout who returns to school later in life to achieve their dreams. Begin the story with the phrase "It was never too late." Avoid using the phrase "too late" in the opening sentence.
response:
It was never too late.

The rain battered the windows of *St. Jude's University*, a rhythmic drumming that usually sent Mark drifting into a fog of regret and self-doubt. Yet, today, he stood at the front door with his hands buried deep in the pockets of a worn leather jacket, clutching a stack of application forms and a trembling envelope. The label on the letter read: "Application for Late Entrance - Fall Term."

Years ago, at age eighteen, Mark had packed his books away just as easily as if they were mere paperweights. He wanted to be an architect, designing skyscrapers that touched the clouds, but life had other plans. A car crash claimed his parents, leaving him a ward of the state and suddenly responsible for two younger sisters. Money ran out, dreams evaporated, and the silence of the house became heavier than any brick. He traded his dream sketches for part-time jobs at the grocery store, learning the language of barcodes instead of blueprints.

Now, at forty-two, the city skyline looked different to him. The same buildings that once mocked his ambition now seemed like unfinished sculptures waiting for their final touches. But the financial scars still stung; the tuition fees could have wiped out his small savings or even forced him into debt he couldn't repay.

"Mark?" The voice broke through his internal monologue. It was Mrs. Gable, the bursar from thirty years ago, who had first enrolled him. She walked over, her silver hair catching the lamplight, looking almost gentle. "I heard you were coming back."

"I tried once," Mark whispered, avoiding her eyes. "In college. Right after my father passed. I couldn't afford it. Couldn't take care of them. I told myself I'd come back someday when everything was ready. And then... nothing happened."

"You aren't alone, Mark," she said softly. "You are not bringing your past with you."

She handed him a cup of coffee, steam curling around her hands. "But don't bring your fear. You are here now. You have your own story. Your father built a house before he passed, didn't he? It's standing strong today. What you are about to build will do the same."

Mark took a sip, the warmth spreading through his chest. He thought about his sisters, who were now grown women living safe lives because he was there. He thought about the blueprints he kept hidden under his mattress, yellowed with age but undiminished in spirit. They weren't just drawings anymore; they were a promise kept to himself and those he loved.

"The classes start tomorrow," Mrs. Gable continued. "Architecture 101. Don't worry about missing history. Don't worry about being caught up. Just walk in, drop off these forms, and pick up a pencil."

Mark nodded, the knot in his stomach finally loosening. He looked at the heavy oak door leading inside, a place that felt alien yet utterly familiar. He took a deep breath, smelling the old dust and fresh paint of the lecture halls.

He pushed the door open and walked in. The noise of the campus rushed toward him—shuffling feet, chatter, the rustle of papers—but to Mark, it sounded like a symphony of possibility. He adjusted his coat, stepped onto the hallway floor, and found his path forward.

It truly had been never too late.<|im_end|>
